risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomization and packaging was organized by an independent statistician at the host site, using the SPSS software Rand function. The sites received batches of 90 envelopes as required throughout recruitment, each consisting of five blocks of 18 random assignments (seven NRT, seven bupropion, four NRT + bupropion)." Comment: This describes a computer-based random allocation sequence generated by an independent statistician, with random assignments in blocks. This is sufficient to judge that a proper random sequence was used.

Quote: "To allow participants to be recruited at satellite centres without computer links, randomization was by sealed envelope. Numbered envelopes containing a random treatment allocation were provided to each site. On enrolment participants selected their envelope from a large batch and signed it before breaking the seal to reveal their allocation." Comment: This explicitly describes a concealment mechanism that prevents foreknowledge of the next assignment at enrollment. The numbered sealed envelopes provided from a batch, opened only after selection and enrolment, meet allocation concealment criteria sufficiently to judge low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was an open-label parallel-group trial" and "we could not include placebos to allow a no-treatment condition or to blind treatment allocation". Comment: Participants and personnel were not blinded to assignment, and the trial explicitly states that blinding was not implemented. Because key outcomes included smoking cessation and self-reported unwanted symptoms/adherence, knowledge of treatment could plausibly influence these outcomes, so performance bias is high.

Quote: "Blind follow-ups were not considered feasible, due to patients usually discussing their treatment when contacted." Comment: Outcome assessment for smoking cessation relied on self-report plus expired-air carbon monoxide validation, and the objective CO measure is unlikely to be influenced by lack of blinding. The outcome assessor was not blinded at follow-up, but the key primary/secondary cessation outcomes were CO-verified and therefore largely objective, so detection bias from unblinded assessment is unlikely.

Quote: "The two follow-ups for the primary outcome (session 7, 26 weeks) were completed by 659 (61.5%) participants and this rate did not differ significantly between treatment groups (χ2(2) = 3.33, P = 0.19). Of the 412 participants not completing both follow-ups, 241 (58.5%) reported smoking at one follow-up which they did complete... This gave a primary outcome completion rate of 84%. Again, this rate did not differ between treatment groups." Comment: Although complete follow-up was not achieved, attrition for the primary smoking cessation outcome appears balanced across groups and most missing participants were identifiable as smokers at one time point, limiting the likelihood of differential attrition bias. The study also used an ITT analysis with those not attending follow-up considered relapsed/continuing smokers, which is a plausible and conservative approach in this context.
